title: Control plane anatomy
summary: Five core components run the cluster: kube-apiserver, etcd, kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, and (in cloud clusters) cloud-controller-manager. Know exactly which does what.
points: **`kube-apiserver`** — the only component that talks to `etcd`. Every `kubectl` call, controller, and `kubelet` watch goes through it. It is the cluster's hub and the one piece that horizontally scales easily.

**`etcd`** — distributed, strongly consistent key-value store using the Raft consensus algorithm. Holds the entire cluster state. Production clusters run an odd number of members (typically 3 or 5) to tolerate failures; lose quorum and the cluster goes read-only.

**`kube-scheduler`** — watches for pods with `spec.nodeName` empty, then picks a node based on resource requests, taints/tolerations, affinity, topology spread, and pod priority. It only writes the binding; it never starts containers.

**`kube-controller-manager`** — a single binary running many controllers (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Job, EndpointSlice, ServiceAccount, etc.). Each controller runs a reconcile loop driving actual state toward desired state.
**`cloud-controller-manager`** — splits cloud-specific logic out of the core: provisioning `LoadBalancer` services, attaching disks, and updating Node objects when the underlying VM goes away.
**Control plane vs. nodes** — `kubelet` and `kube-proxy` run on every worker node, not on the control plane. On managed services like EKS/GKE/AKS, the control plane is hidden from you entirely.
Modern Kubernetes uses the term **control plane**, not 'master' — that label was deprecated in 1.20 and removed from defaults.
traps: `etcd` is the source of truth, not a cache. Restoring from an `etcd` snapshot restores the cluster.
Only `kube-apiserver` reads/writes `etcd`. Controllers and the scheduler never touch it directly.
The scheduler does not run pods — it only assigns them to a node. The `kubelet` on that node is what actually starts containers.

title: Workload controllers: pick the right one
summary: `Deployment`, `StatefulSet`, `DaemonSet`, `Job`, `CronJob` — each solves a different shape of workload. The exam trades on picking the right one for a given description.
points: **`Deployment`** — stateless, interchangeable replicas managed via a `ReplicaSet`. Supports `RollingUpdate` (default) and `Recreate` strategies. Use for web apps, APIs, stateless workers.

**`StatefulSet`** — stable network identity (`app-0`, `app-1`), ordered startup/teardown, and per-pod `PersistentVolumeClaim` via `volumeClaimTemplates`. Use for databases, Kafka, Zookeeper — anything where pod identity matters.
**`DaemonSet`** — exactly one pod per matching node, scheduled even on nodes the scheduler would normally skip. Use for log shippers (Fluent Bit), CNI agents, `node-exporter`, kube-proxy itself.
**`Job`** — runs pods to successful completion with `completions` and `parallelism` knobs. Pods are not restarted on success; failed pods are retried up to `backoffLimit`.
**`CronJob`** — creates `Job` objects on a cron schedule. Time zone defaults to the controller's UTC; use `spec.timeZone` (stable since 1.27) for explicit zones.
**`ReplicaSet`** — the low-level controller that maintains replica count. You almost never create one directly; `Deployment` owns it for you.
traps: 'Runs on every node' is always a `DaemonSet` hint, never a `Deployment` with `replicas: <node count>`.
`StatefulSet` pods get predictable ordinal names; `Deployment` pods get a hash + random suffix. If a question shows `app-0`/`app-1`, it's a `StatefulSet`.
A `Job` runs once to completion — it is not the same as a `CronJob` running once. `CronJob` schedules `Jobs` repeatedly.
Deleting a `StatefulSet` does NOT delete its PVCs by default — that's intentional, to protect data.

title: Service types: ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer vs ExternalName
summary: Four `Service` types, each with different exposure. The exam loves to ask 'which type would you use for X?'.
points: **`ClusterIP`** (default) — stable virtual IP reachable only inside the cluster, implemented by `kube-proxy` rules (iptables, IPVS, or nftables). Pod-to-pod and pod-to-service communication.
**`NodePort`** — allocates a port from the `30000–32767` range on every node and forwards to the Service's `ClusterIP`. External clients hit `<any-node-IP>:<nodePort>`.
**`LoadBalancer`** — superset of `NodePort`. The cloud-controller-manager (or MetalLB on bare metal) provisions an external L4 load balancer that targets the NodePort on each node.
**`ExternalName`** — pure DNS `CNAME` to an external hostname. No proxy, no `ClusterIP`, no selector, no endpoints. CoreDNS just returns the alias.
**Headless Service** — `clusterIP: None`. DNS returns the pod IPs directly instead of a virtual IP. Required for `StatefulSet` per-pod DNS (`app-0.svc.namespace`).
traps: `NodePort` is not 'node-level' — it's just a port opened on every node that forwards to pods chosen by the selector.
`LoadBalancer` requires cloud integration. On bare metal it stays `<pending>` forever unless you install MetalLB, kube-vip, or similar.
`ExternalName` has no `ClusterIP`, no endpoints, and no selector — it is purely a DNS construct.
`Service` is L4 (TCP/UDP/SCTP). For HTTP host/path routing you need `Ingress` or Gateway API on top.

title: Probes: liveness, readiness, startup
summary: Three probe types with different jobs. Mixing them up is a classic exam trap.
points: **Liveness probe** — 'is the container still healthy?' On failure, the `kubelet` kills and restarts the container according to its `restartPolicy`.

**Readiness probe** — 'is the container ready to serve traffic?' On failure, the pod is removed from the `EndpointSlice` of any matching `Service` (no restart). Useful for warm-up and temporary back-pressure.
**Startup probe** — gates liveness and readiness during slow boots. While the startup probe runs, the other two are paused. Once it succeeds, control hands over.
**Probe handlers** — `httpGet`, `tcpSocket`, `exec`, and `grpc` (stable since 1.27). Tune `initialDelaySeconds`, `periodSeconds`, `failureThreshold`, and `timeoutSeconds` per app.
A pod with no readiness probe is reported `Ready` the moment its container starts — often too eager for apps that need to warm caches or open DB connections.
traps: Readiness failure does NOT restart the pod. It only removes the pod from Service endpoints.
Liveness probes kill containers — set them conservatively. An aggressive liveness probe on a slow-starting JVM is a famous self-inflicted outage.
If a startup probe is defined, the liveness probe is disabled until startup succeeds. That is the whole point.
Probes run from the `kubelet` on the pod's node — they hit the pod IP directly, not via a `Service`.

title: ConfigMaps and Secrets
summary: Both inject configuration into pods. `Secrets` are base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default — encryption is an opt-in `kube-apiserver` feature.
points: Both can be consumed as environment variables (`env` / `envFrom`), as command-line args via `$(VAR)` substitution, or as files via a `volumeMount`.
Files mounted from a `ConfigMap`/`Secret` update on the node when the object changes (with kubelet sync delay, ~60s). Environment variables are set at pod start and never refresh.

**`Secret` is base64, not encryption.** Anyone with read access to the API can decode it. To protect data at rest, enable `EncryptionConfiguration` on `kube-apiserver` (AES-CBC, AES-GCM, or KMS provider).
Both have a 1 MiB size limit per object (etcd value-size constraint).
`Secret` types include `Opaque` (default), `kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson` (image pulls), `kubernetes.io/tls`, and `kubernetes.io/service-account-token`.
Use `immutable: true` on `ConfigMap`/`Secret` to prevent accidental edits and reduce kubelet watch load.
traps: '`Secrets` are encrypted' is wrong by default — base64 is encoding, not encryption.
Env vars from `ConfigMap`/`Secret` do not hot-reload — you must restart the pod (or use a tool like Reloader).
RBAC on `Secret` is what actually protects them. A user with `get secrets` can read every value.

title: Pod networking: every pod gets an IP
summary: Kubernetes networking model: a flat L3 network where every pod gets its own IP and any pod can reach any other pod without NAT.
points: Each pod gets a unique cluster-routable IP. Containers in the same pod share the network namespace and reach each other on `localhost`.
Pod-to-pod traffic crosses nodes without NAT — this is a hard requirement of the Kubernetes networking model.
The **pause container** (a tiny process that just sleeps) holds the network namespace so app containers can crash and restart without losing the IP.
**CNI plugins** implement the actual networking. The `kubelet` calls the configured CNI on pod creation/deletion to plumb the network. Common plugins: Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave, AWS VPC CNI.
**Service IPs** are virtual — they live in `kube-proxy` rules (iptables/IPVS/nftables), not on any interface. That's why you can't ping a `ClusterIP`.
**DNS** is provided by `CoreDNS` running as a `Deployment` in `kube-system`. Pods resolve `svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local`.
traps: Two containers in one pod cannot listen on the same port — they share a single network namespace.
'Containers in a pod share an IP' is true; 'pods share an IP' is false.
You cannot ICMP ping a `Service` `ClusterIP` — it has no listener, only `kube-proxy` rules.

title: The autoscaler trio: HPA vs VPA vs CA
summary: Three different autoscalers operating on three different axes. Don't confuse them.
points: **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** — scales the replica count of a `Deployment`/`StatefulSet`/`ReplicaSet` based on CPU, memory, custom metrics, or external metrics. Built into the controller-manager.

**Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)** — adjusts pod CPU/memory `requests` and `limits` based on observed usage. Modes: `Off` (recommend only), `Initial`, `Auto`. In `Auto` it evicts and recreates pods to apply changes.
**Cluster Autoscaler (CA)** — adds nodes when pods are unschedulable due to resource pressure and removes nodes that have been underutilized for a configurable period.
**HPA needs metrics** — install `metrics-server` for CPU/memory, or a custom adapter (Prometheus Adapter, KEDA) for custom metrics.
**Karpenter** is an alternative to Cluster Autoscaler on AWS that provisions right-sized nodes directly from EC2 rather than scaling node groups.
**KEDA** (event-driven autoscaling) extends HPA to scale on external signals (Kafka lag, queue depth, cron) and supports scale-to-zero.
traps: HPA and VPA both targeting the same resource (e.g., CPU) on the same workload conflict — use VPA in recommendation mode or scale on a different metric.
Cluster Autoscaler does not scale based on node CPU/memory utilization. It scales based on pending pods that cannot be scheduled.
HPA never sets `requests`/`limits`. VPA does that. CA never touches pods — it adds/removes nodes.
Without `requests` set on pods, HPA cannot compute CPU utilization correctly.

title: NetworkPolicy: resource vs enforcement
summary: `NetworkPolicy` is a Kubernetes API object, but the CNI plugin actually enforces it. If your CNI doesn't support it, the policy is silently ignored.
points: **Default behavior** — pods with no `NetworkPolicy` selecting them allow all ingress and egress.
**Selecting a pod** — once any policy with a matching `podSelector` exists for a direction (ingress or egress), that pod becomes 'isolated' for that direction and only the allowed traffic passes.
**Rules are additive** — multiple policies are OR-ed together. There is no explicit `deny`; you express deny by omission.

**Enforcement varies by CNI** — Calico, Cilium, Antrea, and Weave enforce `NetworkPolicy`. Stock Flannel does NOT — apply a policy and it is silently ignored unless you pair Flannel with Calico (canal) or another enforcer.
**Scope** — `NetworkPolicy` is namespace-scoped. For cluster-wide rules, use vendor CRDs like `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` or Calico's `GlobalNetworkPolicy`.
**L4 only by default** — standard `NetworkPolicy` selects on namespace, pod labels, IP blocks, and ports. L7 (HTTP method, path) requires service-mesh or CNI extensions.
traps: Applying a `NetworkPolicy` with stock Flannel and expecting enforcement — silent failure.
There is no explicit deny rule. Default-deny is achieved by an empty-rule policy that selects all pods.
DNS often breaks when locking down egress — remember to allow UDP/TCP 53 to `kube-dns`/`CoreDNS`.

title: Storage: PV, PVC, StorageClass, CSI
summary: Storage abstraction in three layers. Admins provision `PV`s (or a `StorageClass`); users claim them with `PVC`s; CSI drivers do the real work.
points: **`PersistentVolume` (PV)** — cluster-scoped resource representing actual storage (EBS volume, NFS share, Ceph RBD). Lifecycle independent of any pod.
**`PersistentVolumeClaim` (PVC)** — namespaced request for storage. Bound to a `PV` matching size, access mode, and class. A pod references the `PVC`.

**`StorageClass`** — blueprint for dynamic provisioning. A `PVC` referencing a class triggers the CSI driver to create a `PV` on demand. Mark one as default with `storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"`.
**Access modes** — `ReadWriteOnce` (one node), `ReadOnlyMany`, `ReadWriteMany`, and `ReadWriteOncePod` (single pod, stable since 1.27).
**Reclaim policy** — `Delete` (destroy the underlying volume when the PVC is deleted) or `Retain` (keep it for manual recovery).
**CSI** (Container Storage Interface) is the standard plugin model — every modern storage backend ships as a CSI driver. The in-tree volume plugins are deprecated/removed.
**`volumeClaimTemplates`** in a `StatefulSet` creates one `PVC` per pod ordinal, giving each replica its own persistent disk.
traps: `ReadWriteOnce` means one node, not one pod. Multiple pods on the same node can share an RWO volume — use `ReadWriteOncePod` to truly restrict to a single pod.
A `PVC` without a `StorageClass` and no default class binds only to pre-created PVs (static provisioning).
Deleting a `PVC` with reclaim policy `Delete` destroys the underlying cloud volume. Production PVs should usually be `Retain`.

title: Ingress and Gateway API
summary: `Ingress` is the original HTTP router for Kubernetes. Gateway API is the successor — more expressive, role-oriented, and multi-protocol.
points: **`Ingress`** — L7 routing rules (host/path → `Service`). Just a config object: it does nothing without an Ingress controller (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Contour) installed and watching.
**`IngressClass`** — selects which controller handles a given `Ingress`. Multiple controllers can coexist by class.

**Gateway API** — newer CRD-based standard, GA for `Gateway`/`GatewayClass`/`HTTPRoute` since 1.29. Separates concerns by role: `GatewayClass` (infra provider), `Gateway` (cluster operator), `HTTPRoute`/`TCPRoute`/`GRPCRoute` (app dev).
Gateway API supports cross-namespace routing via `ReferenceGrant`, weighted traffic splitting natively, and protocols beyond HTTP.
Neither resource provisions a load balancer on its own — the controller behind it does (e.g., NGINX controller exposes itself via a `LoadBalancer` Service).
**TLS termination** — `Ingress` references a TLS `Secret` per host; Gateway API uses listeners on the `Gateway` object.
traps: An `Ingress` resource with no controller installed does nothing. Creating the YAML is not enough.
Don't mix up `Ingress` (L7 HTTP/HTTPS) with `Service` of type `LoadBalancer` (L4, any TCP/UDP).
Ingress is feature-frozen (no new features) — non-trivial routing should use Gateway API or controller-specific annotations.
Gateway API is not part of core Kubernetes — you install the CRDs and a conformant controller separately.

title: Cloud native principles: 12-factor and immutability
summary: The mental model behind the whole CNCF ecosystem: stateless, declarative, immutable, automatable.
points: **Immutable infrastructure** — never mutate a running server; rebuild the image and replace the instance. Containers and `Deployment` rolling updates embody this directly.
**Declarative APIs** — describe desired state in YAML/JSON; controllers reconcile actual state to match. Imperative `kubectl create` exists, but the GitOps world is declarative.
**Stateless apps** — state lives in backing services (databases, object stores, caches), not on the compute node. Pods are cattle, not pets.
**12-factor highlights** — config in env vars, treat logs as event streams to stdout/stderr, disposable processes (fast startup, graceful SIGTERM), dev/prod parity, single codebase per app.
**CNCF definition of cloud native** — 'scalable applications in modern, dynamic environments' built with containers, service meshes, microservices, immutable infrastructure, and declarative APIs.
**Loose coupling and microservices** — small services with explicit APIs, deployed independently, communicating over the network.
traps: 'Cloud native' does not mean 'in the cloud'. It means built using cloud-native patterns — perfectly fine on-prem.
Containers alone are not cloud native. A pet container with manual `docker exec` patches is not cloud native.
Microservices are an option, not a requirement. A well-designed monolith can still be cloud native.

title: Service mesh: what it gives you
summary: A service mesh layers observability, mTLS, and L7 traffic control onto service-to-service calls — usually via sidecar proxies.
points: **Common features** — mTLS between services (zero-trust), retries with timeouts, circuit breaking, request-level metrics and traces, traffic splitting, header-based routing.
**Architecture** — *data plane* (proxies like Envoy or `linkerd2-proxy` next to each pod) + *control plane* (configures the proxies, distributes certs).
**Istio** — feature-rich, Envoy-based, complex. Supports both sidecar mode and *Ambient mode* (sidecarless: per-node `ztunnel` for L4, optional waypoint proxies for L7).
**Linkerd** — lightweight, opinionated, ultralight Rust proxy. Easier to operate; fewer knobs.
**Cilium Service Mesh** — eBPF-based, can run sidecarless by leveraging the kernel; integrates with Cilium CNI and `NetworkPolicy`.
**Consul Connect** — HashiCorp's mesh, multi-runtime (works beyond Kubernetes).
traps: A mesh does not replace Kubernetes Services or DNS — it runs alongside them, intercepting traffic via iptables/eBPF redirection.
Sidecars add real overhead — CPU, memory, latency. Sidecarless modes (Ambient, Cilium) trade architectural complexity for resource savings.
mTLS in a mesh is between proxies, not between application processes. Apps still see plaintext on `localhost`.

title: CNCF landscape: who does what
summary: The 'which project lives in which category' grid is where most KCNA candidates lose points. Memorize the categories and the graduated projects in each.
points: **Orchestration & scheduling** — `Kubernetes` (the only one that matters for KCNA).
**Container runtime (CRI)** — `containerd`, `CRI-O`. Docker is no longer a Kubernetes runtime: dockershim was removed in Kubernetes 1.24 (2022).
**Networking (CNI)** — `Calico`, `Cilium` (graduated 2023), `Flannel`, `Weave Net`, `Multus`.
**Storage (CSI)** — `Rook` (Ceph operator), `Longhorn`, plus countless vendor CSI drivers.
**Service mesh** — `Istio` (graduated 2023), `Linkerd` (graduated 2021).
**Observability** — `Prometheus` (metrics, graduated 2018), `Jaeger` (tracing), `Fluentd`/`Fluent Bit` (logs), `OpenTelemetry` (unified signal pipeline), `Cortex`/`Thanos` (long-term Prometheus).
**App delivery / GitOps** — `Argo` (ArgoCD/Rollouts/Workflows, graduated 2022), `Flux` (graduated 2022). Packaging: `Helm` (graduated 2020).
**Service proxy** — `Envoy` (graduated 2018).
**Registries & supply chain** — `Harbor` (registry), `in-toto`, `Notary`, `TUF`, `SPIFFE`/`SPIRE` (workload identity).
**Maturity levels** — *Sandbox* (early), *Incubating* (proven adoption), *Graduated* (mature, broad adoption).
traps: Prometheus is metrics ONLY. Not logs, not traces.
OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral signal pipeline (SDKs + Collector), not a backend — it feeds Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo, and the like.
Helm is packaging, not GitOps. Argo CD and Flux are GitOps controllers that often consume Helm charts.
`Docker Engine` is not a Kubernetes runtime any more, but `containerd` (which Docker uses internally) absolutely is.

title: Observability: three pillars, three tools
summary: Metrics, logs, and traces. Know the canonical CNCF project for each, and how OpenTelemetry sits across all three.
points: **Metrics** — aggregated numerical samples over time. `Prometheus` is the de facto standard: pull-based, scrapes `/metrics` endpoints in the text exposition format, queries with `PromQL`. Stores time-series in a local TSDB.

**Logs** — discrete events with structured context. `Fluentd` and `Fluent Bit` collect and forward; `Loki` indexes by labels (Prometheus-like) rather than full text; `Elasticsearch`/`OpenSearch` index everything.

**Traces** — request paths across services with parent/child spans. `Jaeger` and `Zipkin` are the long-standing backends; `Tempo` is Grafana's trace store. Sampling is essential — full tracing is too expensive.

**OpenTelemetry (OTel)** — unified, vendor-neutral instrumentation for all three signals. Replaces the older OpenTracing + OpenCensus projects. Ships SDKs per language and a `Collector` that receives, processes, and exports.
**Exemplars** link metrics to traces — a histogram bucket can carry a trace ID so you can jump from 'p99 spiked' to the exact slow request.
**`metrics-server`** is for HPA/`kubectl top` only — short-lived, in-memory, low cardinality. It is not Prometheus.
traps: Prometheus is not for logs. A classic exam trap.
Grafana is a visualization layer, not a storage backend. It reads from Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, Elasticsearch, etc.
`metrics-server` ≠ Prometheus. The former feeds HPA; the latter is a full TSDB.
OpenTelemetry doesn't store anything by itself — you still need a backend (Prometheus, Jaeger, Tempo).

title: GitOps: pull vs push
summary: Git is the single source of truth for desired cluster state. An in-cluster controller continuously reconciles actual state to match the repo.
points: **Four GitOps principles** (OpenGitOps): declarative, versioned and immutable, pulled automatically, continuously reconciled.
**Pull model** — agent inside the cluster watches the repo and applies changes (`Argo CD`, `Flux`). Safer because the cluster never needs an inbound credential exposed to CI.
**Push model** — CI pipeline runs `kubectl apply` from outside. Simpler to set up, but CI must hold cluster credentials and there is no continuous drift detection.
**Argo CD** — UI-heavy, `Application` CRD per app, strong RBAC and audit trail, sync waves and hooks.
**Flux** — composed of small controllers (`source-controller`, `kustomize-controller`, `helm-controller`, `notification-controller`); lighter footprint, GitOps-Toolkit style.
**`Helm` and `Kustomize`** compose cleanly with both — Argo CD and Flux can render charts/overlays before applying.
**Drift detection** — both tools constantly compare cluster state to Git and either alert or auto-revert manual changes.
traps: GitOps means Git is the source of truth and a controller reconciles it. 'We store our YAML in Git' alone is not GitOps — the reconciliation loop is what defines it.
Jenkins or GitHub Actions running `kubectl apply` is push-based CI/CD, not GitOps in the OpenGitOps sense.
Argo CD and Flux are not CI tools — they don't build images. Pair them with a CI system that updates Git with new image tags.

title: Deployment strategies: rolling, blue-green, canary
summary: Three strategies for shipping a new version, with different blast radius, infra cost, and rollback speed.
points: **Rolling update** (Kubernetes `Deployment` default) — replace pods gradually, controlled by `maxSurge` (extra pods allowed above desired) and `maxUnavailable` (how many can be down). No extra infrastructure required.
**Recreate** — `strategy.type: Recreate` kills all old pods before starting new ones. Brief outage; required when old/new versions cannot coexist (e.g., schema lock).
**Blue-green** — two full environments side by side. Cut over traffic at once (Service selector swap or LB switch). Instant rollback, but doubles the infrastructure during the window.
**Canary** — route a small percentage of traffic to the new version, observe error/latency, then ramp. Needs a traffic-splitting layer (service mesh, Gateway API weights, or `Argo Rollouts`).
**Progressive delivery** — automating canary/blue-green decisions based on metrics; tools include `Argo Rollouts` and `Flagger`.
**Feature flags** complement these strategies: ship code dark, then enable per-user/percent without redeploying.
traps: Rolling updates give progressive pod replacement but no traffic-weight control — a pod is either in the Service or out, all-or-nothing per pod.
Canary needs an intelligent router; a `NodePort` or plain `ClusterIP` Service cannot do weighted splits — it round-robins endpoints.
Blue-green doubles the cluster footprint during the cutover window. Budget for it.
Setting `maxUnavailable: 0` and `maxSurge: 0` together makes a `Deployment` undeployable — at least one must be non-zero.

title: Container runtimes and the CRI
summary: The `kubelet` does not run containers itself — it talks to a CRI runtime over a Unix socket. Knowing the dockershim story is exam-critical.
points: **Container Runtime Interface (CRI)** — the gRPC contract between `kubelet` and the runtime. Lets Kubernetes support multiple runtimes without code changes.
**`containerd`** — the most widely deployed runtime. Originally extracted from Docker; CRI support is built in via the `cri` plugin.
**`CRI-O`** — a minimal runtime built specifically for Kubernetes by the OpenShift community. No daemon beyond what CRI needs.
**Low-level runtime** — both `containerd` and `CRI-O` shell out to an OCI runtime (`runc` by default) to actually create the Linux namespaces and cgroups for each container.
**Sandboxed runtimes** — `gVisor` (`runsc`) and `Kata Containers` (lightweight VMs) are drop-in OCI runtimes for stronger isolation.

**Dockershim removal** — the in-tree shim that let `kubelet` talk to Docker Engine was removed in Kubernetes 1.24 (April 2022). Clusters using Docker Engine must switch to `containerd`, `CRI-O`, or use the cri-dockerd shim from Mirantis.
**Images are still OCI** — containers built with `docker build` work fine on `containerd`/`CRI-O`. The image format is independent of the runtime.
traps: 'Docker is removed from Kubernetes' is half right. Dockershim is gone; `containerd` (the engine inside Docker) is alive and well as a runtime.
Docker images and OCI images are interchangeable. Switching runtime does not require rebuilding images.
`runc` is not a CRI runtime — it's the OCI low-level runtime that the CRI runtime calls.

title: RBAC and ServiceAccounts
summary: Role-Based Access Control gates every API call. `ServiceAccount`s are how pods (and CI/CD) identify themselves to the API.
points: **`Role` / `ClusterRole`** — define a set of allowed verbs (`get`, `list`, `create`, `delete`, etc.) on resources. `Role` is namespace-scoped; `ClusterRole` is cluster-wide.
**`RoleBinding` / `ClusterRoleBinding`** — bind a Role/ClusterRole to a subject: a `User`, `Group`, or `ServiceAccount`.
**`ServiceAccount`** — a namespaced identity for processes running in pods. Every namespace has a `default` SA, mounted into pods automatically unless `automountServiceAccountToken: false`.
**Token projection** — modern Kubernetes (1.22+) uses bound, time-limited projected tokens via `TokenRequest`, not the legacy long-lived `Secret`-based tokens.
**Verbs are additive; deny is implicit** — RBAC has no deny rule. If no binding allows the action, it is denied.
**Aggregated ClusterRoles** — `ClusterRole`s with `aggregationRule` automatically merge in matching child roles, used heavily by operators to extend `view`/`edit`/`admin`.
traps: Pods authenticate to the API as their `ServiceAccount`, not as a human user. CI tools that `kubectl apply` typically use a kubeconfig tied to a SA token.
There is no deny rule in RBAC. The only way to remove access is to not grant it — or use admission controllers (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) on top.
`cluster-admin` is not a magic `*` flag — it's a `ClusterRole` like any other. You can audit it with `kubectl describe clusterrole cluster-admin`.

title: Scheduling: requests, limits, QoS
summary: Resource `requests` decide where a pod runs; `limits` cap what it can use. The combination yields a QoS class that drives eviction order.
points: **`requests`** — the amount of CPU/memory the scheduler reserves on a node. Sum of requests on a node cannot exceed allocatable.
**`limits`** — the cap. Memory over the limit triggers an OOMKill; CPU over the limit is throttled (CFS quota), never killed.

**QoS classes** (assigned automatically by the kubelet):
 - `Guaranteed`: every container has equal `requests` and `limits` for both CPU and memory.
 - `Burstable`: at least one container has a request, but not all match limits.
 - `BestEffort`: no `requests` or `limits` set.
**Eviction order under node pressure** — `BestEffort` first, then `Burstable` exceeding requests, `Guaranteed` last.
**CPU is compressible, memory is not.** Throttle vs. kill is a frequent exam distinction.
**`LimitRange`** sets per-namespace defaults; **`ResourceQuota`** caps total namespace usage.
**Scheduling primitives** — `nodeSelector`, `nodeAffinity`/`podAffinity`, `taints`/`tolerations`, `topologySpreadConstraints`, and `priorityClass`.
traps: Without `requests`, the scheduler treats CPU/memory as zero — pods can land on overcommitted nodes and starve.
Memory limits cause OOMKills; CPU limits cause throttling. Different failure modes, different debugging.
Taints repel pods unless they tolerate; node affinity attracts pods. Don't conflate the two.
`QoS = Guaranteed` requires `requests == limits` for **both** CPU and memory on **every** container in the pod.

title: Helm and Kustomize
summary: Two different ways to manage Kubernetes manifests: Helm templates with values; Kustomize layers patches over a base. They solve overlapping problems with different tradeoffs.
points: **Helm** — a package manager. A *chart* is a directory of templated YAML plus a `values.yaml`; rendering produces manifests. Releases are tracked server-side in a `Secret` per namespace.
**Helm templates** use Go template syntax. Powerful (loops, conditionals, functions) but harder to lint statically — the templates only become valid YAML after rendering.
**Helm hooks** run jobs at lifecycle points (`pre-install`, `post-upgrade`). Useful for migrations.
**Kustomize** — built into `kubectl` (`kubectl apply -k`). A *base* of plain YAML plus *overlays* of strategic-merge or JSON patches. No templates, no DSL.
**Kustomize features** — `commonLabels`, `commonAnnotations`, `namePrefix`, `images:` (tag override), `configMapGenerator`, `secretGenerator`, `replicas`, and `patches` for surgical edits.
**They compose** — Argo CD and Flux can render Helm charts and then post-process with Kustomize (or vice versa). It's not either/or.
traps: Helm v3 has no Tiller and no cluster-side daemon — that was Helm v2. Anything mentioning Tiller is outdated.
Kustomize is part of `kubectl`, but the standalone `kustomize` CLI is often more current — `kubectl`'s embedded version lags.
Helm chart values are merged by the chart's logic; you cannot patch arbitrary fields the chart doesn't expose. Kustomize can patch anything.
`helm template` renders locally without contacting the cluster — useful for piping into other tools.
